item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disturbing, this SCP only reaffirms my disdain for monarchy and royal families in general +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was easily one of the most disturbing imagery I've seen in a while, it was just amazing. One can clearly see how much effort was put into this. At first I wasn't sure about actually interviewing 3288, as it kind of was against the obscure vibe I was getting, but by the end I think it was a wise decision, and maybe necessary to the pacing too. This is a very easy +1 for me, and probably one of my favorites articles as well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I get that this is supposed to be a fictional horror story which takes some inspiration from European history. I do however want to point out that this portrayal of the habsburgs and their attitude is very, very unhistorical. Does this make the article bad? No. Will I downvote for this reason? Definitely not. This article does exactly what it's supposed to do.

But… what kind of history nerd would I be if I didn't at least point out where this article takes some (huge) liberties with actual history?

Ignore this if you aren't in the mood for a very, very long, pedantic rant about history
Prepare for a LOT of boring historical trivia

First of all, the stories of Habsburg inbreeding are, well, not exactly wrong, but definitely exaggerated. What you need to know is that marriage between first or second cousins was seen as perfectly normal in all noble families in Europe for centuries, not only the Habsburgs, though they were certainly a particularly notable case.

What you need to know about historical and medieval rulers is, that they had a very very peculiar worldview, in which both your religious duties and your familial duties determined whether you were a good or a bad person/ruler. Whereas religious norms always were interpretable and a little flexible, the concept of family always had had a very strong and decisive influence on the politics of Europe.

An example: In the Holy Roman Empire, the Emperor was not born - like the kings of England or France - but they had to go through a more difficult process.. When the King or Emperor died, the nobles of the Empire would assemble and nominate a new King, the King of the Romans. The King of the Romans would then travel to Rome, where the Pope would either declare them Emperor or not. Often these Roman Kings would have to wage wars against the Pope for decades, or risk their lives and finances in crusades, in order to force or convince the Pope to bestow upon them the title of Emperor. Why did they risk this? They were already King, why would they want to be Emperor so badly?

First of all, the title of Emperor was prestigious. In theory, de jure, the Roman Emperor was a direct successor to both Charlemagne and Augustus Caesar, and therefore also the heir to the Roman Empire. Furthermore, the Holy Roman Emperor was technically the ruler over all catholic nations, including Spain, France, England etcetera. However, this was all just theory. The one time an Emperor actually tried to enforce this claim (Heinrich of Luxembourg) the French and English just rolled their eyes and ignored him. Emperors also didn't really have any powers that the Kings of the Romans didn't have - with one exception: They were allowed to nominate their sons as successors. Technically, if there is an Emperor of the Romans, then the title of King of the Romans is vacant again. Many of the earlier Emperors pressured their nobles and forced them to make an oath that they would be loyal to the Emperor's son and elect him as the next king.

Now some of the more historically inclined readers may wonder why I am writing all of this. After all, almost everything I have written above was obsolete by the time the Habsburgs were around. Emperor Maximilian I abolished the idea of Papal coronation somewhere around 1500 after all. However, I want to illustrate something here:

Heinrich IV was a King of the Romans from the Salier dynasty in the early middle ages. Before him, his father was King, and his grandfather before him. Despite the fact that the Empire was an elective monarchy, the sons of the previous king were often perceived as more "legitimate" candidates and more likely to be elected. However, Heinrich IV was confronted with a disloyal nobility who started to doubt this line of reasoning and wanted to emphasise the elective nature of the system. The King was seen more as a "Primus Inter Pares" than as a divinely chosen leader. As if that wasn't enough, the Pope and Heinrich also couldn't stand each other. For his entire life, Heinrich IV fought against the nobles and various aristocratic rebellions on the one side, and the Pope on the other side. His goal was to secure the throne for his son and to gain the title of Emperor to continue the rule of the Salier dynasty as Emperor. He fought multiple wars, was excommunicated, and risked his life in countless battles, only for his son.

And you know what?

He didn't raise him. Like many kings and nobles in the middle ages, Heinrich IV travelled through the country, instead of residing at a capital. His children were raised by his most loyal vassals and advisors. Many medieval rulers risked their lives and everything they had, for children that they sometimes never met. In fact, Heinrich IV died when he was betrayed by his son who sided with the nobility.

What does this have to do with the Habsburgs? Two things:

1.)As the above example demonstrates, families were a really, really important business in medieval times. Families didn't really exist in the modern sense - many parents only knew their children as adults, since they couldn't be bothered raising them. Nevertheless, the worldview of the medieval world meant that many people would risk their lives for these people that they didn't know, simply because they knew the same blood was flowing through their veins.

Another example (a shorter one, I promise): Sigismund of Luxembourg, Emperor of the Romans, King of Bohemia, Moravia, Croatia and Hungary. During the time of Sigismund, the dynasty of Luxembourg had a strong hold on the title of Emperor, both Sigismund's brother Wenceslaus and his grandfather Heinrich VII had been Emperors. However, Sigismund had no legitimate male children. When it became clear, that his many titles couldn't be passed down to a legitimate male heir, Sigismund decided something that might seem alienating to us today: He didn't declare one of his illegitimate sons to be the heir. Nor did he make his daughter his heir. He made his son-in-law, Albrecht of Habsburg, his heir. After his death, Albrecht became king of Bohemia, Croatia, Hungary, Archduke of Austria and Emperor of the Holy Roman Emire. Albrecht was Sigismund's son-in-law and also risked his life fighting the Bohemian Hussites alongside his father-in-law, despite frictions between the Austrain Habsburgs and Bohemian Luxembourgs in the past.

What this means, is that generally, family relations were the strongest guarantee for a long-lasting and stable alliance in the middle ages. Even if you had never met your ally in your whole life, and didn't really get along with them anyway, you could still count on the fact that they would probably not betray you - simply because they were related to you, even if it's just an in-law-relation. There are exceptions to this of course - like Richard Lionheart/John Lackland, or Charles V/Ferdinand I, or Heinrich III/Richard of Cornwall. However, in all of these cases, ultimately, the family relations were more important than whatever political struggles there might have been for a time. For the longest time, a marriage was like a seal and signature at the end of a contract.

Therefore, if you were a ruler between 1000 and 1800, and you wanted to make sure that somebody was on your side and would help you out in times of war, you should marry them. Incest is bad, sure, but many rulers found sex with their cousin preferable to dying alone in a hopeless war without any allies. A non-Habsburg/German example, that might be more well known in the Anglosphere: The Tudors. Henry Tudor was technically a member of House Lancaster, whereas his wife was a York. Both Houses descended from the children of Edward III, and while I am to lazy to look at the family tree right now, I am pretty sure that this would have made them third cousins or something. Henry seems to have found the marriage preferable to a civil war though.

2) Coming back to Heinrich IV of the Holy Roman Emperor, or more specifically to his family - the history of the Holy Roman Empire can generally be divided in three parts. First: The early and high middle ages, where the Kings of the Romans were elected and crowned by the Pope. During this time, the Empire was huge and included parts of France among others. There were no real rules as to who was allowed to vote for the Emperor. Ultimately, the nobles usually chose the son or another close family member of the previous King/Emperor. During this time, there were three Imperial dynasties: The Liudolfinger (who founded the Empire), then the Saliers (descendants of the last Liudolfing Emperor's third cousin) and the Staufers (descendants of the last Salier Emperor's brother-in-law). Each dynasty ran into more problems than the last, as the nobles began to really understand the implications of an Imperial election. While the rule of Staufer Emperors, especially Barbarossa and Friedrich of Sicily is generally seen as the "Golden Age" of the Empire, it was also the first time when the nobles played with the idea of electing somebody completely unrelated and different instead - in fact there were two short interregnums during the Staufer era, when Lothar of Saxony and Otto of Welfen, two non-Staufer nobles were elected as kings.

After the death of Konrad IV, the last Staufer, the nobles finally had won. During the Late Middle Ages, the nobles didn't care anymore about familial relations and just started electing whoever the hell they thought would be a good and strong Emperor instead of going by dynasty. During this time also, the electors were finally defined: Three arch-bishops in the west, as well as the Count of Brandenburg, the Palatinate and the King of Bohemia - those were the new electors. Rudolf of Habsburg was a small count from a small swiss county. He was the first one who was elected as King by the new Electoral College. His family, the Habsburgs would go on to be rivals for the throne with the family of Luxembourgs and the family of Wittelsbach. Since family relations were no longer seen as a valid justification for a claim to the Imperial crown, the three families instead attempted to build larger regional bases of power inside the Empire. Rudolf gave his family fiefs in the South-East, thereby founding a new large regional power, Austria. Meanwhile, the Luxembourgs inherited Bohemia, and later Hungary. The Wittelsbacher ruled over Bavaria and the Palatinate. These three big regional powers would now directly use their influence to convince the electors. Generally, the Bohemian Luxembourgs were by far the most successful, especially Sigismund, who at the height of his power, just commanded all of Christianity to convene and solve an inner-catholic schism. However, after his death, Sigismund, as mentioned before, passed his titles to his son-in-law, a Habsburg. Albrecht of Habsburg, now as ruler of Bohemia, Austria, Croatia and Hungary, also became Emperor and started the third part of Holy Roman History: The part where for 400 years, only the Habsburgs would be elected as Emperor.

Which brings us to the infamous inbreeding of the Habsburg families. Allegedly responsible for all kind of deformations and genetic defects, though, to be honest, Rudolf I, the first prominent Habsburg Emperor might already have had some of them, specifically the famous chin. Why did the Habsburgs do that? Well, first of all, there were different lines of the Habsburg family. In Austria, the Archdukes of Austria, who were taking care of the internal politics, weren't always closely related to the Habsburg Emperors and Kings, which dealt on a more international scale. Furthermore, Austria was a young country. The old duchy of Austria, as led by the Babenbergs was just one of many duchies and for a long time had been under Bohemian control, the "New, Improved" Austria of the Habsburgs was relatively young. Control over Hungary was lost almost immediately to the Hunyadi families, and when the Habsburgs eventually took control of it again, half of Hungary was under Turkish control. Bohemia, meanwhile, had a problem with the Hussite reformation and was unwilling to accept a catholic Austrian. For many years, Czech and Polish kings ruled over Bohemia, so that the Habsburgs were left with nothing but Austria and an unstable Empire that was a unified country in theory only. The Austrians had enemies in Bohemia (the Hussites), Hungary (the Hunyadis) and beyond (THE superpower of the Late Middle Ages, the Ottoman Empire). So the Habsburgs had to resort to their famous strategy: If we cannot beat them, we'll marry them. Okay, so they didn't marry the Turks or the Hussites, obviously, but they did marry the prestigious Burgundians and the Portuguese, then the Spanish and various smaller Italian and German nations. And it worked out great: not long after the Habsburgs had lost control over Hungary and Bohemia, they had in exchange placed themselves on the thrones of Burgundy (including the Netherlands and Belgium), as well as Spain.
Charles V was a Habsburg, King of Burgundy and Spain, as well as Holy Roman Emperor. His brother was Austrian Archduke, King of Bohemia, Croatia and Hungary again. The Habsburgs were a huge political power, that ruled over some of the most prestigious nations of the world. The only problem was that the rest of Europe hated the Habsburgs: France hated the Holy Roman Empire because they both wanted to rule the Holy Roman/Burgundian counties of Lorraine and Franche Comte, the English weren't happy with the Habsburgs because the unpopular former Queen, Mary the Catholic, was Half-Habsburg; the Ottomans didn't like Austria either, and the Bohemian subjects were still somewhat unhappy about Catholicism. Plus, there suddenly was a man in the Empire who made some very dangerous statements about the Church. This "Martin Luther" called for a rebellion against the Pope, but technically, the Holy Roman Emperor was still the Protector of the Catholic Faith. In short: The Habsburgs needed to keep what allies they had, no matter the cost.
After Charles V resigned, his brother Ferdinand became Emperor, Archduke of Austria, King of Bohemia, Hungary and Croatia. Meanwhile, the son of Charles V, Felipe, became King of Spain, the Netherlands and Belgium.
This is the time of the so-called Habsburg-French Dichotomy. The Habsburgs as rulers of the Empires were the foes of the French. Since the French were one of the most powerful and old nations in Western Europe, and unlike Austria, didn't have to deal with the Ottomans in the South East, the Habsburgs had always been at a disadvantage. Ferdinand knew that his nephew Felipe was ruling in Spain, which was good - it meant that France was surrounded in the South and East, and less dangerous to the Austrians. His son Maximilian II was only a cousin of the Spanish King - not a good guarantee for an alliance. To secure the continued collaboration of the Spanish and Austrian Habsburgs, Maximilian married his cousin Maria of Spain and married their daughter to Felipe. Now he was the father-in-law, brother-in-law and cousin of Spain. The close familial relations between the spouses was gross, sure, but without them, the Austrian and Spanish Habsburg lines would have eventually separated and the alliance against the French would have been in danger. And indeed, the combined efforts of Spanish and Austrian Habsburgs was the only reason that the two countries survived the Thirty Years War against France as well as they did. The Habsburgs continued these marriage politics for a few decades, to always ensure that the rulers of Spain and Austria were always obligated to help each other. Eventually, they did the same with France - Louis XIV was half-Habsburg and yet, Louis XVI married a Habsburg.

So why all the incest? Realpolitik. A marriage was a guarantee for a stable alliance. The Habsburg needed to know for sure that their territories and titles would remain in the possession of a Habsburg. To loose a title means to loose a territory, money, soldiers,… - and with the amount of powerful enemies that the Habsburgs had collected over the centuries, loosing a territory was synonymous to requesting an invasion. Ultimately it were both the loss of Spain and the desperate, but exhausting and unsuccessful wars to regain it, that sealed Austria's decline from THE major power of Europe to Napoleon's doorstopper.

The way this article portrays the Habsburgs is very inaccurate. A desire to "keep the bloodline fresh" was not at all the reason for the inbreeding. It was a result of political conflicts, and probably more of a "necessary evil" than a goal. By the way, the great-grandson of Leopold I, Jakob II, was known for a series of highly modern and idealistic reforms that would have made Austria one of the most progressive and liberal states of his time. He is one of the rulers usually considered to have followed the concept of enlightened absolutism, alongside Friedrich the Great of Prussia. You would probably know him as one of the greatest and most progressive and fair rulers of all time - if it wasn't for the fact that his naive idealism completely failed and none of his reforms outlasted his reign. His mother, the famous Maria Theresia was a very devout Christian by the way. Neither would have gone along with what SCP-3288 would have done. Jakob in particular would have refused to see the peasants as "meat".

Incidentally, Austria and Spain, the "Habsburg countries" were the first ones to actually ban cousin-marriage, according to Wikipedia.

…Oh boy this got a little out of control. Will anybody ever read this? Oh and this is on page 6 of the discussion threat, isn't it? Nobody will ever see this. Great.

Way, Way Too Long; Didn't Read:

The Habsburgs emerged as a great European power under risky circumstances. From the beginning, Austria had enemies in the Empire, as well as in Bohemia, in Hungary, the Ottoman Empire and they were the arch-enemies of the very powerful kingdom of France for 400 years.
Before 1800 marriages guaranteed stable alliances. The Habsburgs needed to keep these alliances for multiple centuries because of their many enemies.

+ show block
– hide block

Marriages at this time worked a little like conquest. There were two things a ruler could do with their daughters: Marry them off to an unmarried potential ally to secure an alliance and to get a chance at inheriting the country ("Offensive"), or marry them off to a land-owning relative to ensure that the family's territories remain inside the family ("Defensive").

Since Albrecht II, the Habsburg followed an "aggressive" marriage strategy, placing themselves on the throne of Burgundy and Spain. Furthermore they attempted to secure the alliances with these countries, England and the Italian nations.

Since Charles V, they tried to maintain these alliances and ensure that the Habsburg territories remained Habsburg territories. The latter basically meant incest - the marriages forged during the "Offensive period" spread the Habsburg blood all over Europe, the marriages in the later "Defensive period" ensured that the Habsburg blood remained all over the continent - through inbreeding, if necessary.

Ultimately, the inbreeding and cousin-marriages were a result of Realpolitik, attempting to keep control over the Habsburg territories. "Keeping the bloodline pure" wasn't the goal - in fact, the later Habsburg rulers would have strongly objected to 3288's worldview (Maria Theresia was too pious, Jakob II and Leopold II were too progressive to be that OTT peasant-hating.)

BTW Austria was the first to ban cousin-marriage in Europe.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please space your paragraphs. Also, the post was still quite long, so I've added another collapsible. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there actually something called Gottschall-Gärtner syndrome?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hooray, the hapsburgs are more creepier then they are! The list of mutations could be a bit shorter tho. +1

item.title: Disgusting and great
item.content:encoded: This was great, I love how it plays up how utterly disgusting and horrible their culture and inbreeding are. The horror it finds in vore is also great, it's a truly grotesque and horrific concept if considered realistically, and perfect for this style of body horror, but most people don't want to touch it with a 50 foot pole, understandably so for the aforementioned grotesqueness and the concepts reputation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s a different Leopold. He’s not even from the same royal family.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering what his son Leopold II did to the Congo, I'm not surprised even a Sarkic priestess would find him disgusting - you know what they say about apples and trees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh, this would be a super fun campaign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh my G-d, this is utterly disgusting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favourite articles on the site. I always liked the stories about deformed cannibalistic people. Funny but on the first sight I thougt they are werewolfes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Habsburgs would be infamous for their generations of inbreeding. That is entirely historical. This also led to mental instability along with intellectual and physical disabilities among its rulers. They had grown so inbred that the family was literally dying out. Wanted to play with the potential horror - you often see the "inbred mutants in the foothills" trope but I wanted to apply it to a people who really viewed themselves as somehow innately superior to all other people, as royals generally did.

This is a great concept with some pretty damn good execution.
My first thought when reading the list of deformities had me thinking they were some sort of ancient inbred tribe of cavemen somewhere, but the buildup and twist just caught me perfectly.
Incidentally, this is one of those SCP entries which one could make into a game; finding and entering one of the ancient vaults in a search and destroy mission. Wouldn't even have to be a videogame either, it could be pen and paper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't the gene have been carried into House Habsburg-Lorraine via Maria Theresa? She was Leopold I's granddaughter.

(Am I asking this because I wanted to see if I can write a tale of the Mayerling Incident in SCP-verse? It's more likely than you think.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this skip, easily one of my favourites. It's remarkably well written and unsettling.

Only thing is, what's the anomaly? This all sounds like normal Habsburg behaviour.

item.title: These make perfect video game enemies
item.content:encoded: Just don't reference the rape stuff, and these things would be perfect to include in a SCP game as fodder enemies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: stomach churningly horrifying, 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …this has gotta be the most fucked up thing I've ever seen on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely also recommend checking out DrChandra's other stories (that I know of) featuring these evil aristocratic goblins: Freaks, Friends and Fiends and the short but wonderfully horrifying Utter Depravity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is a year old, but DrChandra recently wrote a great tale based of of this called Fit For A King, so if you want more monstrous inbreedy goodness, look no further

item.title: Re: Damn it, now I'm terrified!
item.content:encoded:
Come to think of it, poor Rudolf did frequent brothels… Okay, it was because his marriage fell apart. But still.

Perhaps Mayerling was his desperate attempt to die while still human, and by the side of someone he at least cared for [he asked his wife, then a lover, and then the naive Mary Vetsera].

..I have an acute case of the feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Outstanding work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like SCP-1788 combined with SCP-2610 with a touch of outdated values pertaining to royalty and blood purity. Brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, what an incredible article.

Just wanted to say, your description of the entities reminds me of a more horrifying and decadent version of the Crimson Court from Darkest Dungeon, so congrats on giving me nightmares.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have conflicting feelings about the field logs. You've got lines like this:

It wore a crown that had come to more closely resemble a torture device, too small for its head but held in place by an overgrowth of flesh

The tone there is not the least bit clinical, but it's so evocative that I'd be sad to see it removed.

I think the best way to reconcile this would be to move these details into a Tale about the recovery operation, and convert that section of the article into a more clinical summary of the events with a link to the Tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Torture doesn't work that way, though. You get what they think you want to hear, not anything actually true.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One SCP that legitimately scared me. The idea that some mutated, cannibalistic inbred subspecies of humans would one day burst from the ground, and over a few generations take over all of Europe? That's actually scary. Not in a cosmic way (don't get me wrong, I do love myself some lovecraftian horror), but in a way that makes me legitimately fear this being real. The absolutely disgusting description of 3288 mating habits didn't help either. Still, the classification as Keter and not Apollyon, and the fact that 3288s clearly haven't taken into consideration that the world is currently more than 7 billion people strong, makes it likely that the Foundation can prevent it.
Solid execution of body and traditional horror, with a glimmer of hope at the end. +1

Also good to see the Foundation interrogators actually reading the situation for once. Far too many interviews seem to have the Foundation try to threaten the subject, and just give up when they refuse to talk. Playing to a subject's delusions is clearly more effective. King Moser of the Foundation. Heh.

item.title: The harpsichord is the new banjo
item.content:encoded:
This was remarkably well-done. The progression of information here is just fantastic, and I’m glad you resisted the urge to put historical information first- being given a monster and slowly told its origin is something I seem to see less on here than the reverse, and the Memento style of SCP-telling is nice for people like me who read the containment procedures last.

Also, you managed to do remarkably well expressing the ickier elements of the story- neither downplaying them nor focusing on them to the extent of cheapening the macronarrative.

Finally, although the third log was a little less clinical in its execution, it was damn cinematic as presented, and the rest was fairly scientific. I have to admit, the torture at the very end was a little surprising, but it’s better than the Foundation-as-angels vibe that a lot of people try to put out.

+1

item.title: Re: Damn it, now I'm terrified!
item.content:encoded: I like to think that for some (those who continued to be the public face of the Hapsburg's) it wouldn't show at first, but over time, they would start to develop those traits, first mental and later physical - probably beginning with an unspeakable appetite that they try to resist at first ;p (or some might fully embrace as a sort of birthright). Those who didn't want to become like the "other side" of the family, could be driven to desperation and madness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems like an exceptional circumstance, since their interrogation of ALPHA gave them the information that the aristocrats respect and defer to brutality, and become compliant when someone demonstrably has them at their mercy. Their society is based around violent domination where strength is the only respectable value, so in a way torturing them is building a rapport.

item.title: Damn it, now I'm terrified!
item.content:encoded:
Great job - this is the article that led me to finally freaking make a SCP/Wikidot account after ages of lurking.

Also. I live in Vienna. I moved to Vienna because of my love for Elisabeth das Musical. If I wasn't afraid of sewer grates because of Stephen King and Pennywise, I sure as heck am now.

…I'm now left wondering exactly how much of Sisi's behavior and Rudolf's suicidal ideation (which sadly became reality) was because they were affected by this gene. Sisi might be a milder case since she's not directly from the royal bloodline [I think?] but Rudolf got a double whammy with this and the much-vaunted "Wittelsbach madness", not helped by the fact that his parents were cousins. The gene was probably dormant in Franz Joseph…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quack

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Jazz hands*

item.title: Re: I slammed the Austro-Hungarian anthem while reading this
item.content:encoded: Will take that into account.

item.title: I slammed the Austro-Hungarian anthem while reading this
item.content:encoded:
One tiny nitpick as a heraldry enthusiast:

black double-headed eagle against a red backdrop

That colour combination normally not acceptable in heraldry, save for a few fringe cases like Albania, whose national coat of arms is exactly that: a black double-headed eagle on a red field. You could just keep the normal Austrian black eagle on a golden field, or corrupt the ancient coat of arms of the Habsburgs in a similar way: a black lion on a red field or a red lion on a black field.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "So, what's the name of your SCP?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In real life I hold your opinion, but I view the Foundation as quite willing to get their hands dirty. The Aristocrats invoke a lot of disgust and hate. I was thinking of an interrogator snapping as a criminal details the horrific things they've done. I wanted to show that personnel are also human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I can try to improve that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clearly well researched, I googled several disturbing things while reading this, but

The quiet proved short lived, interrupted by the sound of doors creaking open and a growing number of footsteps.

This nonclinical tone ruined it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this up until the final interview. I really dislike torture being presented as instant effective interrogation. The Foundation should really have professional interrogators, and an interrogation that follows effective methods instead would have been a much more interesting narrative, where the interrogator is building report with this subject, even though in the analysis it is clear it disgusts her.

item.title: Re: Hoopsbargs
item.content:encoded: "Daddy, I can't sleep! There's a Hoopsbarg under my bed!"

item.title: Hoopsbargs
item.content:encoded:
Ich bin lederhosen. Ich bin strudel. Ich bin Hapsburg!

+1

P.S. Can we rename the disgusting royalty goblins "Hoopsbargs?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Art I found of 3288-ALPHA with servants!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is one of the best compliments I've ever received :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations on being the second SCP to feature in a dream of mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not just read an elaborate The Aristocrats joke, did I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always thought The Adults was underrated - good stuff. And I'm glad you found it unsettling, I found myself uncomfortable writing it! ;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This kind of reminds me of the Adults (SCP-1788) and it’s unsettling in the same way. Though part of me wants to see a tale where both races’ simultaneous attempts at an SK come into conflict with one another, and soon we’ve got the mob version of a Kaiju battle…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Jesus Christ," the site director says. "What the hell do you even CALL a SCP like that?"

(Yeah, I saw it too. +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fantastic description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I do think the symbolism is clear, I was worried if I pushed it too hard it would come out as hamfisted sermonizing. The use of cliches is done on purpose - I like to take simple anomalies (in this case, what are basically ghouls) and give them an unexpected narrative.

And you are not the first to note that about the flight-recorder. I'm honestly thinking of getting rid of it. I wasn't fully happy about it from the start.

Edit: I also initially intended NOT to use a diary, or at least not lean on it that much, but it was starting to get way to long so I cut it down and stuck to a somewhat straightforward method.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't get into this, I'm afraid.

The initial bullet point description is a checklist of "spooky mutation" cliches, but I will admit that the hard clinical tone there did become effective by the end of the list. The rest of the initial description built up the threat quite well.

The field logs started off slow, and a little predictably, but I was happy to go with that. However the final log both lost the clinical detachment (eg "abruptly", "short lived", most of the physical descriptions) and worked entirely in expected tropes (everything is quiet until suddenly the brave agents realise that the hostiles are everywhere, hostiles don't spot any of the agents who are able to continue observation, brave agent blows himself up after getting eaten).

The interview rather unfortunately reminded me of SCP-3740, which was presumably not the intent. I'm not sure how much the diary really added (or how much I believed it - royal diaries are presumably never truly private, so this seems too obviously revelatory) - it might have been better to pick some of the other documents referred to in this section to do the showing, as the rest of the historical background is quite "tell-heavy".

And then the ending is also very much as expected with threats of the coming darkness from the captive instance. The flight recorder was particularly heavy-handed - it really didn't work for me at all.

The core idea here is strong - class warfare and entitlement leads to monstrosity - but it isn't being illustrated as strongly as it could be. I get the sense that you're falling back a bit on the stuff you know you can do well, rather than finding the elements that will best suit this story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely beautiful and engaging. It feels like you've set a standard for how SCP articles should be written, although the clinical tone was slipping off as it went on - but that's fine because it would all be quite dry without imagery. +1, and long live the House of Habsburg!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…and now Im picturing a cannibals easteregg hunt with things that look like Barlow from Salems lot had a kid with the creatures from Pickmans model, dressed like the Duke of Marlboro, but with huge ass teeth.
Thanks 8-/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No No No, the nobles would soon be checking under the bed for you. Hiding food makes the game of the hunt more fun, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check under your bed for nobles ;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one of the most disturbing things I've read in a while, I have a very vivid mental image of 3288 from the description and the thought of those things lurking underground, coming out at night to feed…
I feel a serious need to check my doors and windows are locked.
Well done +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus christ, from start to finish this made my skin crawl and the more I read the more disturbing it got, definitely a +1 from me….

Now I want this to be a crusader kings mod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Hapsburgs are creepy enough on their own, but combined with cannibalistic rituals and the super-inbreeding of 2610 and you've got a recipe for an article that'll really make the skin crawl, and you executed it masterfully.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bunch of incest, cannibalism, and sexual violence which basically ended with *snap* "We're the Aristocrats". I can't tell whether that was intentional, but have a +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Utterly stupendous. There is nothing about this I didn't like. May this +1 be as a gentle summer breeze on a cold spring morning!
